quiz_questions.question_text: What element is the basis of all organic compounds?

options.text: Carbon is the central element in organic compounds
Nitrogen is the central element in organic compounds
Oxygen is the central element in organic compounds
Hydrogen is the central element in organic compounds

quiz_questions.explanation: Carbon is the basis of all organic compounds. Its ability to form four covalent bonds and bond with itself in long chains makes it uniquely suited to form millions of different compounds.

quiz_questions.question_text: How many covalent bonds can a carbon atom typically form?

options.text: Two covalent bonds per carbon atom
Four covalent bonds per carbon atom
Six covalent bonds per carbon atom
One covalent bond per carbon atom

quiz_questions.explanation: Carbon has four valence electrons and can form four covalent bonds with other atoms, making it extremely versatile in building complex molecules.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is an organic compound?

options.text: NaCl (sodium chloride / table salt)
H2O (water / dihydrogen monoxide)
CH4 (methane / natural gas compound)
CO2 (carbon dioxide / exhaled gas)

quiz_questions.explanation: Methane (CH4) is an organic compound because it contains carbon bonded to hydrogen. CO2 is generally classified as inorganic despite containing carbon.

quiz_questions.question_text: Hydrocarbons are organic compounds made of only:

options.text: Carbon and nitrogen atoms bonded together
Carbon and oxygen atoms bonded together
Carbon and hydrogen atoms bonded together
Carbon and sulfur atoms bonded together

quiz_questions.explanation: Hydrocarbons are organic molecules that contain only carbon and hydrogen atoms. Examples include methane (CH4), ethane (C2H6), and propane (C3H8).

quiz_questions.question_text: What type of bond holds atoms together in organic compounds?

options.text: Ionic bonds between metal and nonmetal atoms
Metallic bonds with delocalized electron seas
Covalent bonds formed by sharing electrons
Nuclear bonds between protons and neutrons

quiz_questions.explanation: Organic compounds are held together by covalent bonds, where atoms share electrons. Carbon forms strong covalent bonds with hydrogen, oxygen, nitrogen, and other carbon atoms.

quiz_questions.question_text: Carbon can form long chains because it can bond to:

options.text: Only one other carbon atom at a time maximum
Other carbon atoms in chains, rings, and branches
Only noncarbon atoms like hydrogen and oxygen
Only metal atoms through metallic bonding forces

quiz_questions.explanation: Carbon atoms can bond to other carbon atoms to form straight chains, branched chains, and ring structures, which is why millions of organic compounds exist.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is the simplest hydrocarbon with one carbon atom?

options.text: Ethane (C2H6) with two carbon atoms
Propane (C3H8) with three carbon atoms
Methane (CH4) with one carbon atom only
Butane (C4H10) with four carbon atoms

quiz_questions.explanation: Methane (CH4) is the simplest hydrocarbon, containing one carbon atom bonded to four hydrogen atoms. It is the main component of natural gas.

quiz_questions.question_text: What are the four most common elements found in organic compounds?

options.text: Carbon, hydrogen, oxygen, and nitrogen
Carbon, sodium, chlorine, and potassium
Iron, copper, zinc, and aluminum metals
Helium, neon, argon, and krypton gases

quiz_questions.explanation: The four most common elements in organic compounds are carbon (C), hydrogen (H), oxygen (O), and nitrogen (N), sometimes remembered by the acronym CHON.

quiz_questions.question_text: Organic compounds are important because they make up:

options.text: Only rocks and minerals found in the Earth's crust
Living things, fuels, plastics, and many everyday materials
Only laboratory chemicals that are not found in nature
Only gases found in the atmosphere of planet Earth

quiz_questions.explanation: Organic compounds are found everywhere: in living organisms (DNA, proteins, carbohydrates), fuels (gasoline, natural gas), plastics, medicines, and foods.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of these is NOT an organic compound?

options.text: Glucose (C6H12O6) found in food
Ethanol (C2H5OH) found in drinks
Table salt (NaCl) found in food
Acetic acid (CH3COOH) in vinegar

quiz_questions.explanation: Table salt (NaCl) is an inorganic ionic compound. It does not contain carbon-hydrogen bonds, which are the hallmark of organic compounds.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is an organic compound?

options.text: Any compound that is produced only in living organisms naturally
A compound that contains carbon atoms bonded to other elements
A compound that is always found as a gas at room temperature
A compound that does not contain any hydrogen atoms at all

quiz_questions.explanation: An organic compound is a chemical compound that contains carbon atoms, typically bonded to hydrogen, oxygen, nitrogen, or other carbon atoms through covalent bonds.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a hydrocarbon?

options.text: A compound made of carbon bonded only to oxygen atoms
A compound made of only carbon and hydrogen atoms
A compound made of hydrogen bonded only to oxygen atoms
A compound containing water and carbon dioxide mixed together

quiz_questions.explanation: A hydrocarbon is an organic compound consisting entirely of hydrogen and carbon atoms. They are the simplest organic compounds and include alkanes, alkenes, and alkynes.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the difference between a saturated and unsaturated hydrocarbon?

options.text: Saturated hydrocarbons have only single bonds while unsaturated have double or triple bonds
Saturated hydrocarbons contain oxygen while unsaturated ones do not contain oxygen
Saturated hydrocarbons are liquids while unsaturated ones are always solid
Saturated hydrocarbons have more carbon atoms than unsaturated ones always

quiz_questions.explanation: Saturated hydrocarbons (alkanes) contain only single C-C bonds and are 'saturated' with hydrogen. Unsaturated hydrocarbons (alkenes/alkynes) have double or triple bonds.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a functional group in organic chemistry?

options.text: A specific group of atoms that gives a molecule its characteristic properties
The total number of carbon atoms present in an organic molecule
A group of molecules that perform the same biological function
The arrangement of electrons in the outermost shell of carbon

quiz_questions.explanation: A functional group is a specific arrangement of atoms within an organic molecule that determines the compound's chemical properties and reactions, such as -OH (hydroxyl) or -COOH (carboxyl).

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a polymer?

options.text: A small organic molecule with only one or two carbon atoms
A large molecule made of many repeating smaller units linked together
A single carbon atom bonded to four different hydrogen atoms
An inorganic compound that contains a long chain of metal atoms

quiz_questions.explanation: A polymer is a large organic molecule made up of many repeating units called monomers linked together in a chain. Examples include plastics, DNA, and proteins.

quiz_questions.question_text: What are carbohydrates in organic chemistry?

options.text: Organic compounds made of carbon, hydrogen, and oxygen that provide energy
Organic compounds made only of carbon and nitrogen that build muscle
Inorganic compounds made of metals that carry oxygen in the blood
Organic compounds made only of carbon and hydrogen for fuel burning

quiz_questions.explanation: Carbohydrates are organic compounds made of carbon, hydrogen, and oxygen (often in a 1:2:1 ratio). They include sugars, starches, and cellulose, and serve as energy sources.

quiz_questions.question_text: What are lipids (fats and oils)?

options.text: Organic molecules that store energy and are not soluble in water
Inorganic molecules that dissolve easily in water solutions
Organic molecules that carry genetic information in the cell
Inorganic molecules that speed up chemical reactions in cells

quiz_questions.explanation: Lipids are organic compounds including fats, oils, and waxes. They are hydrophobic (not soluble in water), store energy, and make up cell membranes.

quiz_questions.question_text: What are proteins in organic chemistry?

options.text: Simple sugars made of only carbon and hydrogen atoms
Large organic molecules made of amino acid monomers linked together
Small inorganic molecules found only in non-living material
Hydrocarbon chains that are used only as fuel for energy

quiz_questions.explanation: Proteins are large organic polymers made of amino acid monomers. They contain carbon, hydrogen, oxygen, nitrogen, and sometimes sulfur, and perform many functions in living things.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is an isomer?

options.text: A molecule that has a completely different formula from another molecule
A molecule with the same formula but a different structural arrangement
An atom that has a different number of neutrons than normal atoms
A molecule that contains only one type of atom bonded together

quiz_questions.explanation: Isomers are molecules that have the same molecular formula but different structural arrangements of atoms, giving them different properties.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the term for a small repeating unit that forms a polymer?

options.text: An isotope of the polymer compound
A monomer unit of the polymer chain
An isomer of the polymer molecule
A catalyst for the polymer reaction

quiz_questions.explanation: A monomer is the small repeating unit that links together to form a polymer. For example, glucose monomers link to form starch (a polymer).

quiz_questions.question_text: A student burns a piece of wood and observes that CO2 and H2O are produced. What does this tell the student about the composition of wood?

options.text: Wood contains only inorganic minerals and no carbon at all
Wood contains organic compounds with carbon and hydrogen atoms
Wood is made entirely of water molecules bonded together tightly
Wood is a pure element that breaks down into simpler elements

quiz_questions.explanation: When organic compounds burn, the carbon produces CO2 and the hydrogen produces H2O. The production of these gases confirms wood contains organic compounds with C and H atoms.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student reads that natural gas (methane, CH4) is a fossil fuel. Why is methane classified as an organic compound?

options.text: Because it was formed from ancient inorganic rocks underground
Because it contains carbon bonded to hydrogen through covalent bonds
Because it is a gas and all gases are classified as organic
Because it is used as fuel and all fuels are organic compounds

quiz_questions.explanation: Methane (CH4) is organic because it contains carbon covalently bonded to hydrogen atoms. Its origin from decomposed organisms also reflects its organic nature.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student learns that DNA is an organic molecule. What elements would the student expect to find in DNA?

options.text: Only iron and copper metal atoms bonded together
Carbon, hydrogen, oxygen, nitrogen, and phosphorus
Only sodium and chlorine atoms in ionic bonds
Only noble gas atoms that do not form chemical bonds

quiz_questions.explanation: DNA is an organic polymer containing carbon, hydrogen, oxygen, nitrogen, and phosphorus. Like all organic molecules, carbon is central to its structure.

quiz_questions.question_text: In a lab, a student tests the solubility of cooking oil in water and finds they do not mix. She then tries mixing the oil with hexane and it dissolves. What does this suggest about oil?

options.text: Oil is an ionic compound that only dissolves in acid solutions
Oil is a nonpolar organic compound that dissolves in nonpolar solvents
Oil is an inorganic compound that only dissolves in metal solutions
Oil is a polar organic compound that dissolves in all types of liquids

quiz_questions.explanation: Cooking oil is a nonpolar organic compound (lipid). It dissolves in nonpolar solvents like hexane because 'like dissolves like,' but not in polar water.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student notices that plastic bags, gasoline, and proteins are all organic. What common element makes them all organic?

options.text: They all contain iron as their main structural element
They all contain carbon as their backbone element
They all contain silicon as their main bonding element
They all contain only hydrogen and nothing else

quiz_questions.explanation: All organic compounds share carbon as the central element in their molecular structure. Carbon's ability to form four bonds creates the diversity of organic molecules.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student is asked why there are millions of organic compounds but far fewer inorganic ones. What is the best explanation?

options.text: Carbon can form chains, branches, and rings, creating endless structural possibilities
Organic compounds are easier to discover than inorganic compounds in nature
Inorganic compounds do not really exist and are only theoretical ideas
Scientists have spent more time studying organic compounds than inorganic ones

quiz_questions.explanation: Carbon's ability to form four bonds and bond to itself in chains, branches, and rings of virtually unlimited length creates an enormous diversity of possible organic structures.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student reads a food label listing glucose, fructose, and sucrose as ingredients. What type of organic compound are these?

options.text: They are all lipids that store long-term energy
They are all proteins made of amino acid chains
They are all carbohydrates that provide quick energy
They are all nucleic acids that carry genetic code

quiz_questions.explanation: Glucose, fructose, and sucrose are all sugars, which are carbohydrates. Carbohydrates are organic compounds made of carbon, hydrogen, and oxygen that serve as energy sources.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student in a lab adds iodine to a food sample and it turns dark blue-black. This indicates the presence of starch. What type of organic molecule is starch?

options.text: A protein polymer made of amino acid monomers
A carbohydrate polymer made of glucose monomers
A lipid molecule made of fatty acid components
An inorganic salt dissolved in the food sample

quiz_questions.explanation: Starch is a complex carbohydrate (polysaccharide) that is a polymer of glucose monomers. The iodine test is a standard method for detecting starch in foods.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student researches biofuels and learns that ethanol (C2H5OH) can be made from corn. Why is ethanol considered an organic compound?

options.text: Because it is only produced by living organisms in nature
Because it contains carbon bonded to hydrogen and oxygen covalently
Because it is a liquid at room temperature like all organic molecules
Because it burns easily and all flammable substances are organic

quiz_questions.explanation: Ethanol is organic because it contains carbon atoms covalently bonded to hydrogen and oxygen atoms. The presence of carbon-hydrogen bonds is the key criterion for organic classification.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student learns that butane and isobutane both have the formula C4H10 but different structures. The student's teacher says they are isomers. How can two compounds have the same formula but different properties?

options.text: They cannot exist since every formula has exactly one structure
The atoms are arranged differently, creating different molecular shapes and properties
One is organic and the other is inorganic despite the same formula
They are really the same compound just measured at different temperatures

quiz_questions.explanation: Isomers have the same molecular formula but different structural arrangements. Butane is a straight chain while isobutane is branched, giving them different boiling points and properties.
